raw_consumer_statement:
I filed three related billing disputes with American Express for unauthorized or duplicate charges placed by XXXX XXXX XXXX, Attorney at Law on XX/XX/XXXX ( {$9900.00} ), XX/XX/XXXX ( {$10000.00} ), and XX/XX/XXXX ( {$10000.00} ). AMEX initially credited the charges but later reversed all three after the merchant resubmitted the same packet of documents.

The merchants packet contained : ( 1 ) old retainer agreements from 2022 that never mention AMEX billing ; ( 2 ) a sworn declaration falsely claiming I owed a {$30000.00} balance and made no other payments ; ( 3 ) two conflicting invoices ( {$41000.00} vs {$30000.00} ) created months after the charges; and ( 4 ) Veritext vendor bills addressed to the merchants law office, not to me. None of these show that I authorized these transactions.

AMEXs denials repeat the merchants claim that no refunds were ever promised, even though AMEX already has two written refund confirmations from the merchant dated XX/XX/XXXX and XX/XX/XXXX. I also provided proof of more than {$160000.00} in prior payments that fully covered his services. AMEX never explained how it determined the merchants inconsistent paperwork outweighed my documented evidence or refund emails.

This violates Regulation Z ( 12 C.F.R. 1026.13 ( h ) ), which requires a reasonable, good-faith reinvestigation of billing disputes. I am requesting that the CFPB direct AMEX to reopen this matter, conduct a senior-level reinvestigation, and explain in writing how it verified the merchants submissions against the contradictory evidence already in AMEXs possession.
